title: Research Statement
text:
My research is motivated by a central question: how can sound theory and rigorous empirical tools assist in designing systems conducive to human flourishing? Addressing the wicked problems of poverty, chronic disease, and extreme inequality requires, in addition to a heavy dose of empathy, an accurate model of reality. I am drawn to issues at the intersection of public health and anti-poverty policy, with a substantive focus on socioeconomic inequality, disability, and the institutional arrangements that shape life outcomes. Across projects, I aim to produce work that is empirically rigorous, transparent in its assumptions, reproducible, and directly relevant to decision-makers.
My training in public policy has pushed me toward an applied model of research, one that values strong research design and attends equally to the practical demands of producing evidence that can support program evaluation, public communication, and stakeholder use. As a result, my work sits at the intersection of analytic research and translational policy work. In practice, this has meant building reproducible data pipelines, conducting quantitative analyses, developing dashboards and accessible reports, and framing findings in ways that connect technical evidence to concrete public problems.



A central example of this approach is my work on the ADA-PARC project at the Nathalie P. Voorhees Center for Neighborhood and Community Improvement at the University of Illinois Chicago. ADA-PARC is a national research effort examining quality-of-life disparities between people with and without disabilities using 2023 American Community Survey data across national, regional, state, and city geographies. As the primary analyst and author, I built a large Census-based analytic pipeline, produced accessible reporting, and developed interactive tools including an R Shiny website and Tableau dashboards designed to translate complex disability-related disparities into formats usable by researchers, advocates, and policymakers alike. The project spans domains including community living, community participation, and work and economic outcomes, enabling a multidimensional view of inequality rather than a narrow focus on any single indicator.
This work has been formative in shaping how I think about policy research. Disability inequality is often discussed in fragmented terms, but the underlying patterns cut across employment, education, transportation, and community involvement in ways that reinforce one another. Working on ADA-PARC deepened my interest in research that can surface where disparities are most severe, how they cluster geographically and demographically, and what kinds of systems appear to shape them. It also reinforced a lesson that is sometimes overlooked in applied work: for findings to be maximally impactful, they must be communicated in a manner that is widely interpretable.



A second strand of my work focuses on public health outcomes and their social determinants. In an independent study project on the correlates of mood disorders, I used CDC health survey data to model the behavioral and demographic predictors of Generalized Anxiety Disorder and Major Depressive Disorder. The analysis distinguished modifiable risk factors including physical activity, sleep, and social connection from non-modifiable demographic characteristics, with the goal of identifying more actionable entry points for prevention-oriented policy.
What interested me most in this project was not the modeling exercise itself, but the underlying tension it surfaces: much of what drives mental health outcomes at the population level reflects social conditions and institutional arrangements, yet prevention policy is often designed with a focus on downstream individual behavior. That tension between structural context and individual risk is one I find consistently important across policy domains. Excellent policy design, particularly in the realm of public health, identifies the critical intervention point farthest up the causal chain. It asks not just how to handle poor outcomes, but how to prevent them from occurring in the first place.



My interest in moving from description to evaluation is reflected in a program evaluation project on the Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC). Working with two collaborators, I applied a regression discontinuity design using synthetic data structured around WIC’s income-eligibility threshold to estimate potential effects on infant birth-weight outcomes. The project included a program logic model, a causal identification strategy, and robustness checks including bandwidth sensitivity and placebo tests. Using synthetic rather than administrative data was a deliberate methodological choice that allowed us to demonstrate the full evaluative framework under realistic structural constraints, a common challenge in applied policy research settings.
The project sharpened my understanding of how quasi-experimental methods can support credible inference when randomization is not available, a situation that characterizes most policy evaluation. More broadly, it reinforced my commitment to asking not only what is happening, but whether specific policies and institutional arrangements are actually reducing the inequities they are designed to address.



Taken together, these projects reflect the kind of work I hope to continue building. I am most interested in research roles where I can contribute to rigorous applied analysis on questions of physical and mental health, inequality, and social policy, and where the expectation is not only to analyze data, but to help shape how evidence is interpreted and used. I am drawn to policy labs, think tanks, and applied research organizations because they offer a setting where methodological discipline and clear communication are both valued, and where findings are expected to reach audiences beyond academic journals.
Methodologically, I want to keep strengthening my skills in quantitative analysis, program evaluation, and reproducible research workflows, with particular attention to causal inference methods and spatial analysis of inequality. Substantively, I hope to build deeper expertise in how public systems shape economic security, health, and social inclusion.
In the longer term, I intend to pursue doctoral study in public policy, human development, or a closely related field. My aim is to develop a research program oriented around a central question: how do social conditions such as poverty, instability, and constrained opportunity shape health, psychological well-being, and long-term life trajectories, and to what extent can upstream policy interventions disrupt those pathways? I am drawn to work that bridges applied econometrics and the social determinants of health, using tools like quasi-experimental design and longitudinal data to evaluate how programs that strengthen the basic conditions of life including income, housing, and healthcare affect individual and communal well-being over time. Ultimately, I want to produce scholarship that makes inequality more legible, more solvable, and more central to public decision-making, grounded always in the conviction that the precursors to flourishing are owed to every human being.

title: WIC Program Evaluation
text:
The Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC) has served low-income mothers, infants, and young children since 1972, providing access to nutritious food, nutrition education, and healthcare referrals. As of 2024, WIC benefits nearly 53% of all infants born in the United States. Despite its scale, credible causal identification of its effects on birth outcomes remains methodologically challenging due to the absence of randomized assignment. This project demonstrates how quasi-experimental methods can generate policy-relevant evidence under realistic data constraints by applying a Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD) to the program’s income-eligibility threshold, exploiting the discontinuity at the cutoff to identify the effect of WIC participation on infant birth weight.
Key Findings: Using both optimistic and pessimistic synthetic data scenarios, the RDD analysis finds statistically significant treatment effects in both specifications. The pessimistic scenario produces an estimated effect of −498.5 grams (95% CI: −550.9 to −446.1; z = −18.6, p < 0.001) and the optimistic scenario yields −661.9 grams (95% CI: −839.4 to −484.6; z = −7.3, p < 0.001), with results stable across bandwidth sensitivity checks. The consistency across scenarios and robustness checks demonstrates the analytical framework’s reliability as a model for applied program evaluation. As the primary goal of the project was methodological demonstration using synthetic data, the findings are interpreted as a proof-of-concept for the RDD approach rather than as causal estimates of WIC’s real-world effect.
Methods: Sharp Regression Discontinuity Design exploiting WIC’s income-eligibility threshold (185% FPL); optimistic and pessimistic synthetic data scenarios; local polynomial regression estimation; robustness checks including bandwidth sensitivity and placebo threshold tests. Implemented in R.
Collaborators: Colin Heeneman, Emily Frost, Samantha Sepulveda. Course: PA 528 Dr. Siciliano, University of Illinois Chicago.

title: ADA‑PARC Project
text:
ADA-PARC (Americans with Disabilities Act Participation Action Research Consortium) is a national research effort led by Dr. Joy Hammel at the University of Illinois Chicago. The project examines quality-of-life disparities between people with and without disabilities using American Community Survey (ACS 5-year) data, spanning national, regional, state, and city geographies. The analysis covers four thematic domains, Demographics, Community Living, Community Participation, and Work & Economics, producing scorecards, geographic breakdowns, and index scores designed to surface where disparities are most severe and how they cluster across place and population.
Key Findings: Employment gaps between people with and without disabilities are universal and large, with 30–40 percentage point gaps common across states and no state approaching parity. Labor force exclusion, rather than unemployment alone, is the dominant driver, with the share of working-age adults with disabilities not in the labor force frequently exceeding 30%. Income gaps are similarly consistent, with median individual income for people with disabilities lower than for people without disabilities in every state examined, many gaps exceeding $15,000–$20,000 annually. Poverty disparities appear in every state and every age group with no reversals, with working-age PWD poverty approaching 25% in many states and exceeding 30% in others. Geographic patterning is evident throughout: outcomes are systematically worse in Southern states and better in Upper Plains and Mountain West states on employment, and in Mid-Atlantic and Western Pacific states on wealth and poverty indicators.
Methods: Descriptive statistics and composite index scoring using ACS 5-year estimates (2023), with an equity lens centering comparisons between people with disabilities (PWD) and people without disabilities (PWOD) across race, sex, age, and geography. Delivered through a written report, an interactive R Shiny website, and Tableau dashboards.
Role: Primary analyst and author of report. Responsible for overhaul of Census data pipeline construction, index development, report production, and interactive tool development in R Shiny and Tableau.
Explore the ADA‑PARC Interactive Tool →

title: Projects
text:
Portfolio Projects
Each project listed below links to a dedicated page with an overview, methodology, and the full report.



ADA‑PARC Project


A descriptive analysis of quality‑of‑life disparities between disabled and non‑disabled populations across U.S. geographies, paired with an interactive Shiny website and Tableau dashboard.




Correlates of Mood Disorders


Statistical analysis of CDC survey data identifying modifiable and non‑modifiable predictors of depression and anxiety, with implications for public health policy.




WIC Program Evaluation


A regression discontinuity design evaluation using synthetic data to estimate the causal impact of WIC on infant birth‑weight outcomes.




Medicaid Expansion & Health Coverage


A staggered difference-in-differences analysis using the Callaway-Sant’Anna estimator to estimate the causal effect of ACA Medicaid expansion on uninsured rates and cost-related care avoidance across U.S. states.

Uses CDC health survey data to model how modifiable behaviors and non‑modifiable demographics predict depression and anxiety among adults, revealing actionable public health targets.

Applies a regression discontinuity design to synthetic data to estimate the impact of the Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC) on birth weight outcomes.

title: About
text:
Why I’m Committed To This Work
Empathy has always been a central force guiding my personal, academic, and professional life. For as long as I can remember, I’ve been deeply saddened and perplexed by the continuation of such egregious socioeconomic inequality. My mother had premonitions that justice-oriented work was on the horizon when I would do such things as saving half my sandwich for someone seeking donation at Union Station. But such acts felt less in the spirit of heroism than basic humility. It feels only fitting, now, to make that kid proud by dedicating my professional life to understanding and combating the structures which have given rise to our current reality, one in which we are poised to mint our first trillionaire before eliminating abject poverty.
However ironically, precisely because such a state of affairs is a policy choice rather than dystopian prophecy, there exists the hope of its amelioration. We must simply open our eyes, and summon our wits, our optimism, our courage, to steadfastly push for a more just society. A society which truly takes seriously its capacity to foster or destroy the conditions which give each individual the opportunity to flourish. My commitment to this worldview sharpened during my undergraduate studies in political science at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. Here, was the genesis of my ability to link my innate compassion with the tools of rigorous analysis. Such an approach guided my advocacy as part of Active Minds, a student mental health organization for which I served as Co-President my senior year. A role of profound meaning, it deepened my understanding of public service and affirmed my will to to develop systems which minimize human suffering.
After graduating, I wanted to delve deeper into the policy world and expand my knowledge-base in systems which support the well-being of communities. Hence, the next two years in my academic journey were spent pursuing an MPP at The University of Illinois Chicago with a specialization in Public Health. During my coursework here, I developed an appreciation for the framework of upstream intervention. An empathic society makes great effort to prevent problems rather than just manage their symptoms. Another highlight of my graduate experience was the mentorship of Dr. Allyson Holbrook, under whom I completed an independent study using National Health Interview Survey (NHIS) data to explore mental health outcomes. More than a project of quantitative analysis, it was a chance to examine how structural factors and the social determinants of health influence well-being at an individual and communal level.
Another domain in which I’ve grown professionally and technically is at the Voorhees Center for Neighborhood and Community Improvement. While here, my main project has been development of the ADA-PARC website, a resource for the disability community and advocates that hosts raw data and analysis related to quality of life outcomes within the disabled community. Strengthening my technical foundation in statistical software and applied data visualization, this work has also brought me closer to a community which experiences severe inequities.
We have, collectively, chosen greed over empathy. No choice poisons the well of humanity more deeply. For such extreme inequality not only plunders the spirit of those who face capitalistic-condemnation, it creates a class-chasm that eventually, rips us apart. What we need is a renewal. Of our social contract, yes, but also of our commitment to building systems which realize the human right to self-actualization. My continued exploration of the interface between built socioeconomic environments and individual human development, reflected in the projects throughout this portfolio, is oriented towards expanding our understanding of the pathways to such a society.

title: Correlates of Mood Disorders
text:
Mood disorders, primarily Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) and Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), represent the most prevalent category of serious mental illness in the United States, yet the evidence base for targeted prevention policy remains underdeveloped. This project uses 2022 National Health Interview Survey (NHIS) data from the CDC to model the behavioral and demographic predictors of both conditions, with the goal of identifying where the most actionable policy levers lie. A central motivation is the structural question behind the analysis: to what extent do behavioral and social factors, rather than fixed demographic characteristics, drive mood disorder outcomes, and what does the answer imply for prevention-oriented public health policy?
Key Findings: Modifiable behavioral factors have substantially more predictive power for mood disorder outcomes than non-modifiable demographic characteristics. Sleep quality, physical activity, and social connection emerge as the strongest correlates of both anxiety and depression, suggesting these are the most promising targets for prevention-oriented intervention. Among demographic factors, younger generations show progressively higher mood disorder scores relative to Baby Boomers, females score higher on average than males (with a slightly larger gap on anxiety than depression), and modest but significant racial and ethnic differences exist across the Mood Disorder Index. Alcohol use was not statistically significant in the depression model, and the relationship between work hours and mood outcomes was non-linear. Full-time workers scored better on average than part-time workers, counter to initial expectations.
Methods: Multivariate regression modeling using 2022 CDC NHIS survey data; composite Mood Disorder Index constructed from GAD and MDD indicators; modifiable behavioral predictors (sleep, physical activity, diet, substance use, social connection) modeled alongside non-modifiable demographic controls (age, sex, race/ethnicity).
Role: Sole analyst and author. Independent Study at University of Illinois Chicago, College of Urban Planning and Public Affairs under supervision of Dr. Allyson Holbrook.

title: Medicaid Expansion & Health Insurance Coverage
text:
The Affordable Care Act’s Medicaid expansion created one of the most studied natural experiments in U.S. health policy. Because the 2012 NFIB v. Sebelius Supreme Court ruling made expansion optional, states chose to expand at different times, or not at all, for reasons largely independent of underlying health trends. This staggered adoption allows credible causal identification of expansion’s effect on coverage and access to care.
This analysis uses individual-level American Community Survey (ACS) data and state-level Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) data to estimate two outcomes: (1) the uninsured rate among non-elderly adults aged 18–64, and (2) the share of adults reporting cost-related care avoidance in the past year. The primary estimator is the Callaway-Sant’Anna (2021) staggered difference-in-differences method, which addresses the well-documented negative-weighting bias of canonical two-way fixed effects (TWFE) under heterogeneous treatment timing.
Key Findings: Medicaid expansion reduced the uninsured rate by 2.3 percentage points among all non-elderly adults (95% CI: −3.5 to −1.1 pp) and by 5.1 percentage points among adults below 200% of the federal poverty level (95% CI: −7.0 to −3.2 pp). Event study plots show flat pre-treatment trends followed by an immediate, sustained decline at expansion, the canonical signature of a credible DiD design. Cost-related care avoidance declined directionally post-expansion, consistent with coverage gains translating into improved access, though the estimate does not reach conventional significance thresholds.
Methods: Staggered difference-in-differences using the Callaway-Sant’Anna (2021) estimator; never-expanding states as the comparison group; robustness checks including doubly-robust estimation, exclusion of mid-year adopters, and exclusion of the four largest states. Data: ACS 1-year PUMS (2010–2019) via IPUMS USA; BRFSS (2011–2019).
Role: Sole analyst and author; data pipeline, panel construction, estimation, and write-up.

title: Medicaid Expansion & Health Insurance Coverage

Key Findings: Medicaid expansion reduced the uninsured rate by 2.3 percentage points among all non-elderly adults (95% CI: −3.5 to −1.1 pp) and by 5.1 percentage points among adults below 200% of the federal poverty level (95% CI: −7.0 to −3.2 pp). Event study plots show flat pre-treatment trends followed by an immediate, sustained decline at expansion, the canonical signature of a credible DiD design. Cost-related care avoidance declined directionally post-expansion, consistent with coverage gains translating into improved access, though the estimate does not reach conventional significance thresholds.
Methods: Staggered difference-in-differences using the Callaway-Sant’Anna (2021) estimator; never-expanding states as the comparison group; robustness checks including doubly-robust estimation, exclusion of mid-year adopters, and exclusion of the four largest states. Data: ACS 1-year PUMS (2010–2019) via IPUMS USA; BRFSS (2011–2019).
Role: Sole analyst and author; data pipeline, panel construction, estimation, and write-up.

title: Medicaid Expansion & Health Insurance Coverage
